name: Edmond Hall
abstract: Edmond Hall (May 15, 1901 – February 11, 1967) was an American jazz clarinetist and bandleader. Over his career, Hall worked extensively with many leading performers as both a sideman and bandleader and is possibly best known for the 1941 chamber jazz song "Profoundly Blue".

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic

about_quotes.speaker: Adrian Cunningham
about_quotes.evidence: imitating Edmond Hall, trying to get close to how he would play the melody or how he would solo
about_quotes.doc_title: Adrian Cunningham interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, March 31, 2021

about_quotes.speaker: Ed Polcer
about_quotes.evidence: Herb Hall was the original one, Edmond Hall’s brother, he was there for three years with us.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ed Polcer interviewed by Monk Rowe, Tarrytown, New York, October 31, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: George Wein
about_quotes.evidence: Because the next year I played with Edmond Hall and his quartet at the Savoy.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Wein interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Horace Henderson
about_quotes.evidence: That was my rhythm section. ... Edmond Hall for the other clarinet player.

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Darensbourg
about_quotes.evidence: INTERVIEWER mentions Edmond Hall as someone copied by young guys; INTERVIEWEE does not mention any personal interaction or relationship with Edmond Hall.

about_quotes.speaker: Marty Grosz
about_quotes.evidence: They even use to have those concerts at Birdland. The moldy figs. Here’s Max Kaminsky and Edmond Hall and who else and whatever.
about_quotes.doc_title: Marty Grosz interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, September 4, 1995
